Edition 03 · 2026
03 · Deploy

PR Plan

How to read this document

This is the public relations and thought-leadership strategy for Maxxim, produced in the Deploy phase against Master Briefing v2.1, the Gate 1 decisions of 27 June 2026, and the 1 July refresh that folded in the 29 June product mechanics and the live Jewell partner site. It is the execution blueprint for earned media: the story angles worth telling, the outlets worth telling them to, the founders worth building into recognised voices, and the discipline for protecting the reputation while it is being built. It is the strategy layer. It does not write the final press releases or pitch emails; it defines what those should say and who they should go to. It builds on brand-strategy.html, marketing-plan.html, and discover-summary.html.

Maxxim's PR posture is deliberately founder-led and proof-led, not spend-led. The marketing plan is explicit that Maxxim cannot and need not out-spend horizontal AI players, and that the proof surface and cleared proof do the persuading. So the job of earned media is narrow and specific: build founder credibility, plant the category flag on one owned idea, and create linkable authority that feeds SEO and the partner pitch. It is a thought-leadership programme first and a coverage programme second. The two voices are Clent Jewell, the method owner, architect, and gate-holder, and Raef Akehurst, the engine builder, the product side of the 80/20. Ownership is Clent and Raef only, two owners, no board. Paul Clancy is a Client Partner and business development, not a co-founder, and must never be described as one in any media material.

One rule governs everything here, harder than any other. Proof clearance is strict, and it governs all public Maxxim-brand use. Pottsville Acupuncture is the only launched, cleared public Maxxim proof and the only named Maxxim case study that may appear in any pitch, byline, or interview. OTR Earthmover is internal only. Hidrive may be referenced as percentages only, and only with clearance before public use. Beyond the Clinic, Jewell Tyres, Jewell AI, Estipaint, Assurant, Jewell Health, and Ultimate Labs are internal or demo, clearance not confirmed, and must not be pitched. The live Jewell partner is powerful evidence that the model works, but Jewell's own named results stay under Jewell's banner and are never re-used as Maxxim's own public proof without saying so. Nothing in this plan goes to a single journalist, editor, or platform without Clent sign-off. The briefing is RESTRICTED: no live credentials and no unreleased work appear in any public-facing angle. House style is locked: no em dashes, no exclamation marks, Australian English, confident and plain, never hype, and the business is Jewell, never Joule.

A second discipline carries from the upstream work: what is open stays open. This plan does not invent partner economics numbers, Maxxim's own one-word category lead, the front-door product as settled, the 3 July session promise wording, the fractional CMO model as a Maxxim fact, Maxxim's end-client visibility, or Christy Kilmartin's role split. Where a number or a claim is the founders' to confirm, it is flagged, not filled in. The 3 July session is a private partner discovery and alignment session, not a media event, and is never pitched for coverage.

The idea this whole programme is built to own

Since the 27 June version, the spine of the PR case has gone from a thesis on paper to a working example in market. The human-20 percent and machine-80 percent split, the core of the whole business, is now concretely embodied by the live Jewell partner running on Maxxim. That seam is the most newsworthy thing Maxxim has, and it is what every angle below ladders up to. The framing is drawn from the trust-taste-judgement thesis (Nate B. Jones): as AI commoditises the first 80 percent of knowledge work, the value moves to the human 20 percent, where quality is decided. Maxxim is a business built on exactly that split, and it now has a real operator proving the split works.

The strategic bet the seam encodes is explicit in the product guide and repeated across the brand and marketing plans: good, fast, cheap stops being a trade-off when the human stays exactly where quality is decided. That is a genuinely contrarian, genuinely useful contribution to the defining business story of the moment, and it is the reason a serious journalist would take the call. It is a thesis with a worked example behind it, not a prediction. The PR programme exists to put that thesis, in the founders' voices, in front of the operators, owners, and buyers Maxxim's partners want to reach.

1. PR Objectives

PR is a support act here, not the lead channel. The marketing plan runs earned media as a shared, awareness-stage channel that plants the category flag and lends third-party credibility to a young brand. So the objectives are narrow and specific: build founder credibility, validate the 80/20 seam in third-party voices, and create linkable authority that feeds SEO and the partner pitch. It is a thought-leadership programme first and a coverage programme second, and it is measured against the marketing plan's own PR target, cleared proof and earned placements, not raw volume.

Coverage goals (directional, founders to confirm)

  • Quarter one, the founding-cohort window (July to September 2026): 2 to 3 earned placements, weighted to founder commentary and bylines rather than company news. The 3 July session is a private partner meeting, not a press event, so it is not pitched for coverage. The Maxxim-brand public proof base is one cleared case study, Pottsville, which caps how much hard news there is to tell and is exactly why the emphasis is opinion and thesis, not announcement.
  • Quarter two onward: 3 to 4 placements per quarter as additional cleared case studies land, shifting the mix from pure opinion toward evidence-backed story. This tracks the marketing plan's target of at least two additional cleared Maxxim-brand case studies beyond Pottsville by 31 December 2026.
  • Year-one cumulative: a small, high-quality earned footprint. At least one Tier 1 byline or feature carrying a founder, two to three trade or sector placements, and a steady cadence of reactive expert commentary on AI and the future of agency work.

Share of voice

Maxxim is pre-scale and the category is crowded with louder, better-funded AI-marketing voices. Chasing raw share of voice against them is a losing game and would break the Sage posture the brand strategy commits to. The right target is share of voice on one owned idea: the accountable human in the age of AI, the seam where the machine 80 percent hands off to the human 20 percent. Maxxim should aim to be the name that comes up when an Australian business journalist needs a credible, non-hyped voice on AI and the future of agency work, rather than one more startup shouting about disruption.

Publications worth appearing in, and why

  • Australian Financial Review, particularly the Technology and professional-services beats. Reaches the operators, owners, and corporate buyers who are the partner and end-client audiences across the Gate 1 aperture. A credible AFR presence is worth more to this brand than a dozen startup-blog mentions.
  • Australian marketing and media trade (Mumbrella, AdNews, Marketing Mag). Where the partner audience, agency operators and marketing specialists, actually reads. This is the natural home for the 80/20 seam and the human-moat arguments, and for the new-shape-of-agency story the live partner now grounds.
  • SmartCompany and Startup Daily. The SME-and-founder press, aligned to the capacity-capped operator and the growth-stage end client, and the natural home for the Pottsville outcome story.

Thought-leadership positioning

What the founders should become known for in the media: the most credible Australian voice on the accountable human in the age of AI. Not the loudest predictor of AI transformation, the calmest, most evidence-led explainer of where the value actually moved and why a proven, gated method beats raw model speed. That is a Sage position, and it is exactly the posture the brand strategy commits to. It is also defensible: very few founders can speak to it with a method proven over years, a launched client-owned proof to point at, and a live partner already running the whole model on the same engine.

2. Key Story Angles

Four founder-led angles, each newsworthy on its own terms, each derived from the brand strategy and honouring proof clearance. None is a product announcement dressed as news. The narrative map below shows how they relate: one core thesis, three supporting stories that each prove a different face of it. Every angle respects that OPEN items stay open (no partner numbers, no settled front-door product, no showcase promise) and that Jewell's named results stay under Jewell's banner.

Angle 1: The 80/20 seam, where the value actually went

  • Hook: Everyone is asking what AI will replace. The more useful question is what it made cheap, and where the money moved as a result. The answer: AI made the repeatable 80 percent of knowledge work near-free, and pushed all the value into the accountable last 20 percent, the judgement a named person will stand behind. Clent and Raef built a business on that exact split, and they can show the seam working, the machine 80 percent handing off to the human 20 percent at every gate.
  • Why it is newsworthy: timeliness (AI is the defining business story of the moment) plus novelty (a precise, contrarian, evidence-led framing that cuts against both the hype and the doom). It is a thesis with a worked example behind it, not a prediction. The trust-taste-judgement framing gives it intellectual weight a journalist can build a piece around.
  • Founder voice: Clent leads on the seam and the method, Raef on how the engine is actually built to run the 80 percent through Max, Sal, and Pip. This is the angle that most needs both voices.
  • Target outlets: AFR Technology, Mumbrella, SmartCompany; as a byline first, then as reactive commentary whenever a major AI story breaks.
  • Timing: evergreen, but sharpest when pegged to a major AI product launch, a jobs-and-AI report, or a wave of agency-layoff news. Lead the programme with this one.

Angle 2: The human moat, why a community of operators beats a lone AI

  • Hook: In an age that commoditises the first 80 percent, the durable advantage is not a better model, it is human judgement, and specifically judgement held by a community of trusted operators rather than one person. The moat is people, the trust and taste and judgement that compound across a network, and Maxxim is building it on purpose.
  • Why it is newsworthy: impact and contrarian interest. It reframes the AI debate away from tools and toward trust, taste, and accountability, and argues that the winning structure is a network of humans, not a smarter machine. It speaks directly to the anxiety in the agency and marketing world.
  • Founder voice: Clent. This is the cultural and philosophical argument, the Sage at full strength.
  • Target outlets: Mumbrella and AdNews (agency-world relevance), AFR opinion, marketing and future-of-work podcasts.
  • Timing: peg to AI-and-jobs cycles, agency consolidation news, or any prominent "AI will replace marketers" claim, which this angle answers head on.

Angle 3: The proven method, a launched outcome a client owns, gates enforced by the product

  • Hook: While much of the market ships unaccountable AI output, Pottsville Acupuncture got a full strategy and a launched website it owns outright, delivered through a gated method with a named human signing off at every step. And the gates are not a promise, they are mechanical: the platform unlocks creative only as the briefs it depends on exist and a logo is locked. Proof, not marketing.
  • Why it is newsworthy: impact plus human interest. A real local allied-health business, a tangible launched result, and the owner controls the asset. It grounds the abstract AI debate in one concrete, cleared story, and the product-enforced gate is a fresh, specific detail that separates it from the "we use AI responsibly" chorus. This is the only angle that names a Maxxim client, and the only Maxxim client it may name is Pottsville.
  • Founder voice: Clent, with the Pottsville owner only if they consent and Clent clears it. Named partners and clients are never used publicly without consent.
  • Target outlets: SmartCompany and Startup Daily (the SME-outcome story), regional and local press around Pottsville and the Northern Rivers, allied-health trade if the client consents.
  • Timing: ready now, it is the lead case study. Strongest as the evidence inside Angles 1 and 2 rather than always as a standalone.
  • Clearance note: do not reach for Beyond the Clinic, Jewell Tyres, or OTR Earthmover to bulk this out. Hidrive may be cited as percentages only and only with prior clearance. Jewell's named results stay under Jewell's banner. The single highest-value PR-adjacent action is clearing more named Maxxim-brand proof beyond Pottsville, which widens this angle, and it tracks directly to the marketing plan's proof objective.

Angle 4: The partner-community model, a new shape for the agency, with a live exemplar

  • Hook: Maxxim is not building a bigger agency, it is giving trusted operators the engine to run a proven method under their own name, keep their own clients and margin, and stand behind their own work. A network where the human judgement compounds across the group instead of burning out inside one founder. And it is not theory: a live partner, Jewell, is already running the whole model on the Maxxim engine, the first working proof that the shape holds.
  • Why it is newsworthy: novelty and future-of-work relevance. It is a genuinely different operating model for professional services in the AI era, and it now has a live, named exemplar. The seam is concrete: the partner owns the human 20 percent and the client relationship, the Maxxim engine runs the machine 80 percent, and one named partner owns each client relationship.
  • Founder voice: Clent on the method and the standard, Raef on the platform that makes it scale. Jewell may be described as a live partner running on Maxxim, but its own client results stay under Jewell's banner and are not presented as Maxxim's public proof.
  • Target outlets: AFR professional services and Technology, Mumbrella (the agency-model angle), founder and future-of-work podcasts.
  • Timing: build it after the founding cohort shows early traction, so the story has substance behind it. Do not quote partner economics, those numbers are open. Do not name partners without their consent. The fractional CMO model belongs to Jewell's own packaging and is not stated as a Maxxim fact.

3. Target Media List

Tiered by priority and reach. Australian-first, because the audience, the proof, and the founders are Australian. Specific named publications and the relevant beats are given. Named individual journalists are deliberately not invented here; the approach per outlet is to identify the current AI, technology, marketing, or small-business writer at outreach time and confirm with Clent before any contact.

Tier 1, national and major-reach

  • Australian Financial Review, Technology beat and professional-services and agency coverage. Approach: contributed byline (Angle 1 or 4) and reactive expert commentary on AI. The flagship credibility target.
  • The Australian, Business and Technology sections. Approach: reactive commentary and opinion, pegged to AI-and-work stories.
  • Australian Broadcasting Corporation business and technology programming (radio and online). Approach: expert-source commentary on AI and the future of work, lower-frequency, higher-trust.

Tier 2, industry and trade

  • Mumbrella, advertising, media, and agency-model coverage. The single best fit for the partner audience and the human-moat and 80/20 arguments. Approach: byline plus reactive commentary.
  • AdNews, agency and marketing industry. Approach: commentary and contributed opinion on AI in agencies.
  • Marketing Mag, marketing strategy and practice. Approach: thought-leadership bylines on method and accountability.
  • SmartCompany, SME and startup business press. Approach: the Pottsville outcome story and founder commentary aimed at growth-stage owners.
  • Startup Daily, founder and startup ecosystem. Approach: the model-and-founder story (Angle 4), and AI-for-business commentary.

Tier 3, niche, local, and emerging digital

  • Regional and local outlets around Pottsville and the Northern Rivers (Tweed and regional press). Approach: the local-business-wins story, only with the client's consent. Content focus: a local clinic getting a launched, owned digital presence.
  • Allied-health trade press (relevant to Pottsville's sector). Approach: case-led, only if the Pottsville owner consents to be named.
  • AI, marketing, and future-of-work podcasts and newsletters (Australian-first, founder-friendly). Approach: founder guest spots carrying the 80/20 and human-moat angles. Content focus: long-form conversation where the Sage register works best.
  • Selected LinkedIn newsletters and operator communities where the partner audience gathers. Approach: founder-authored, owned-channel-adjacent, amplifying the same theses. LinkedIn is the primary operator-to-operator surface in the marketing plan.

Validation note. This list is built from the brand and audience strategy, not from live media-database research. Before outreach, validate current beat ownership and contact details per outlet, confirm each target against the founding-cohort timing, and get Clent sign-off on the shortlist. State assumptions to Clent rather than treating any name as confirmed.

4. Thought Leadership Strategy

This is the centre of gravity of Maxxim's PR. The brand is proof-led and the Maxxim-brand public proof base is thin (one cleared case study), so the fastest way to earn authority is consistent, expert founder commentary on a small number of owned themes. One byline does not make a thought leader. The plan is sustained cadence, not a single splash, and it runs on the same fortnightly publishing rhythm the marketing plan sets for content.

Spokesperson positioning

  • Clent Jewell, method owner, architect, and gate-holder. Speaks on the 80/20 seam, the accountable human, the proven method, the mechanical gates, and the partner-community model. Credible because the method has run with real clients for years and produced a cleared, launched, client-owned outcome, and because he now runs the whole model live as a partner. The primary external voice.
  • Raef Akehurst, the engine builder. Speaks on how the platform actually runs the repeatable 80 percent through Max, Sal, and Pip, the product side of the 80/20, and what it takes to build an AI engine a human can stand behind. The technical credibility voice, paired with Clent on the build-focused angles.

No third spokesperson. Paul Clancy is a Client Partner and is not positioned as a founder or company spokesperson in any media context. Named partners are not used publicly without consent, and Jewell's own client results stay under Jewell's banner.

Key topics of expertise

  • The 80/20 seam: what AI made cheap, and where the value moved (trust, taste, judgement).
  • The human moat: accountability held by a community of operators as the durable advantage.
  • The proven method and the mechanical gate: why a standard a human signs, enforced by the product, beats raw model speed.
  • The future of the agency: a community of operators instead of one capped firm, with a live exemplar.
  • Owned outcomes: why clients should own what is built for them, on their own domain, against agency lock-in.

Commentary opportunities

The kinds of news events the founders should be ready to respond to: major AI model or product launches, AI-and-jobs or AI-and-marketing reports, agency layoffs or consolidation, prominent "AI will replace [role]" claims, and any high-profile case of unaccountable AI output causing harm. Each is a natural opening for the accountable-human position, and each is a fast, parallel lane to the slower byline work.

Byline and op-ed targets

Outlets that take contributed content and suit the Sage voice: Mumbrella, AdNews, Marketing Mag, SmartCompany, Startup Daily, and AFR for the strongest single piece. Lead with Angle 1 (the 80/20 seam) as the foundational byline, then Angle 2 (the human moat) as the follow-up.

Speaking engagements and conferences

Worth pursuing once the cohort is moving: Australian marketing and agency conferences, AI-in-business events, and SME and founder forums. Founder talks and panels carrying the 80/20 and human-moat themes. Prioritise rooms where the partner audience sits. Any commitment is Clent's call and must respect that the 3 July session itself is a private partner discovery and alignment session, not a public speaking slot.

The thought-leadership engine

Thought leadership only compounds if it runs as a loop, not a series of one-offs. This is the same compounding-loop logic the whole business runs on. The engine below turns each piece of founder commentary into reusable authority that feeds the next.

5. Press Materials

Lean and proof-disciplined. Every asset honours clearance and house style, and nothing ships externally without Clent sign-off. No live credentials and no unreleased work appear in any asset.

Press release schedule

Maxxim has little hard company news to announce right now and a deliberately quiet, proof-led posture, so formal press releases are rare. The release calendar is tied to genuine milestones only, not manufactured news. Candidate milestones, each Clent's call: a cleared additional case study landing, a named founding-cohort milestone if and only if the partners consent, and the self-serve portal launch much later. The 3 July session is not a release event. When in doubt, prefer a founder byline over a press release; it suits the brand and the Sage voice better.

Media kit contents checklist

  • Company boilerplate in three lengths (from the Copy Deck, ready to lift).
  • Founder bios for Clent and Raef, and headshots.
  • The Pottsville case study, the only cleared public Maxxim proof.
  • A fact sheet of cleared statistics and milestones.
  • Approved messaging on the 80/20 seam, the human moat, and the mechanical gate.
  • Brand assets within the locked identity (Signal Blue #2D5BFF, Poppins, Edition 03, logo usage rules), including the reversed wordmark variant for dark grounds where a dark-background asset is needed.
  • A one-line clearance reminder at the top of the kit: Pottsville only public; OTR Earthmover internal only; Beyond the Clinic and Jewell Tyres internal only; Hidrive percentages only with clearance; Jewell's named results under Jewell's banner.

Company boilerplate

Use the Copy Deck boilerplate verbatim once cleared. The 50-word direction, for reference: Maxxim is the engine that lets trusted operators deliver Jewell's proven 3D Process at scale. Three named specialists, Max, Sal, and Pip, run the repeatable 80 percent of a growth engagement at speed, while a human partner owns the 20 percent clients pay for. Partners license a proven method, keep their margin, and stand behind every result. The engine behind the partner, not the name on the door.

Spokesperson bios

Two bios only. Clent Jewell: method owner, co-founder, and architect of the 3D Process, positioned around the accountable-human and proven-method credibility, and now a live partner running the whole model. Raef Akehurst: co-founder and builder of the platform, positioned around the engineering of an AI engine a human can stand behind. Both state ownership accurately, two owners, no board. Neither should over-claim. Bios are drafted to Clent's approval; the direction is set, the exact wording is his.

Fact sheet

Cleared facts only: the 80/20 split (the engine runs roughly 80 percent through Max, Sal, and Pip, a human owns the 20 percent that pays); mechanical gates that unlock creative only as briefs exist and a logo is locked; Pottsville Acupuncture launched and client-owned; a premium run transacted at around $80,000 within the $25,000 to $250,000 band, no client named; the credited $4,950 Growth Diagnostic entry; gated sign-off at every phase; two owners, no board; Jewell live as a partner on the same engine. No partner economics percentages. No uncleared client names. No live credentials or unreleased work.

Image and asset requirements

Real over illustrated, per the brand imagery rule. Founder headshots, the launched Pottsville site, and structured-output or gate visuals that show the method rather than a black box. No stock AI abstractions of glowing brains or circuit boards; they undercut the human-at-the-wheel position. High-resolution, on-brand, and cleared for the specific use. Dark-ground placements use the reversed wordmark variant within the locked identity.

6. Media Outreach Plan

Relationship-first and respectful of journalists' time and beat. The flow below shows how a story moves from angle to placement across the three tiers, with the exclusivity and approval rules baked in. Every story starts with a clearance check and Clent sign-off; nothing external moves without both.

Approach per tier

  • Tier 1: personalised pitch, often an exclusive offer for the strongest stories. Never a mass press release. Lead with the founder and the idea, not the company.
  • Tier 2: personalised pitch and contributed bylines. This is the byline workhorse tier.
  • Tier 3: personalised but lighter-touch; podcasts via a warm guest pitch, local press via the consented Pottsville story.

Pitch structure guidelines

  • Subject line: the angle in plain words, no hype, no exclamation marks. State the idea, not "exciting news".
  • Hook: open with why this matters to that journalist's beat and readers right now.
  • Supporting detail: the thesis, one cleared proof point (Pottsville only), and the founder available to speak.
  • Call to action: one clear, low-friction ask, an interview, a byline, or a comment.
  • Keep it short, operator-to-operator, and accurate. Never imply open economics are settled, never state the front-door product or the showcase promise as fixed, and never present Jewell's client results as Maxxim's public proof.

Follow-up cadence

One polite follow-up after three to four business days, one more after a week, then stop. No more than two follow-ups. Respecting a non-response protects the relationship for the next pitch.

Exclusivity strategy

Reserve exclusives for genuinely strong stories: a newly cleared case study, or a sharply original thesis piece. Offer the exclusive to the single best-fit Tier 1 outlet first (AFR is the default), hold the rest until it runs or passes, then step down the tiers. Reactive commentary is never exclusive; speed and breadth matter more there.

7. Digital PR and Content

Every earned placement is also a potential backlink and a search-authority signal, which is why PR and SEO are run together here. Maxxim is digital-first and maxxim.ai is the proof surface, so digital PR is not a separate programme, it is the same programme measured a second way. This connects directly to the SEO strategy and the marketing plan's shared-channel design.

SEO-driven PR content (linkable assets)

  • The 80/20 seam as a flagship explainer. A founder-authored, genuinely useful piece on where AI moved the value, hosted on maxxim.ai and pitched as a byline. The kind of reference content other writers cite and link to.
  • The Pottsville case study as a linkable proof asset. A clear, honest write-up of a launched, owned outcome, the natural anchor for any coverage that wants evidence.
  • A future-of-the-agency point of view. The community-model argument as a citable POV piece, supporting Angle 4, with the live partner as the concrete example.

Each links back to maxxim.ai, so earned coverage and owned content compound the same domain authority. Coordinate target topics with the SEO strategy so PR content and search content reinforce, not duplicate. Note the constraint the marketing plan carries: Maxxim's own category lead word is OPEN and is not built into keyword targeting or headlines until Clent settles it; Jewell's external lead word is growth.

Newsjacking approach

Newsjack only where the founders add genuine expert value, never a forced connection. The qualifying test: does Clent or Raef have a real, evidence-led point to make on this story that a journalist would want? If yes, move fast. If it is just a trending topic with a tenuous link to Maxxim, skip it. The strongest natural pegs are major AI launches and AI-and-jobs stories, where the accountable-human position is a real contribution.

Reactive commentary process

  • Who approves: Clent, always. Nothing goes out without his sign-off, including reactive comment.
  • Turnaround target: same day for a breaking cycle, because reactive value decays in hours. A small bank of pre-approved positions on the core themes lets the founders respond fast without re-clearing the underlying thesis each time.
  • Distribution: direct to the relevant beat journalist, plus the founders' own channels.

Social amplification

Maximise the reach of every placement across owned channels: founder LinkedIn (the primary amplification surface for an operator audience), maxxim.ai, and the partner hub. Earned coverage shared by the founders is both a trust signal to the partner audience and a way to extend a single placement's life. House style holds on owned channels too: one sharp true claim, no exclamation marks, emphasis from the claim not the punctuation. Where useful, Pip drafts the amplifying post and a human approves it before publish, which is the model marketing itself.

8. Crisis Communication Framework

Reputations are built slowly and damaged quickly, and a framework written in advance is far more effective than one improvised under pressure. Five realistic risks for this specific business, each with a holding-statement direction. These are frameworks for Clent to finalise, not approved public statements.

Risk scenarioWhat it looks likeHolding-statement direction
1. AI output quality failure An engagement produces flawed, inaccurate, or off-brand AI output that reaches an end client or the public, undercutting the accountable-human promise. Lead with the gate: a named human is accountable at every phase and the gates are mechanical, here is what we are doing to put it right, and here is what the gate is being tightened to prevent it recurring. Own it plainly. Do not blame the model. The whole brand rests on accountability, so the response must demonstrate it.
2. Proof or claims challenge A journalist or competitor questions a claim, or accuses Maxxim of overstating results, misusing a client's name, or passing off Jewell's results as its own. State only what is cleared and true: Pottsville is launched and client-owned, the method has run for years, Jewell's results belong to Jewell as a partner, open numbers are open. Correct any misstatement fast. This risk is contained in advance by strict clearance, which is exactly why clearance is non-negotiable.
3. Client or partner data and confidentiality breach A client's or partner's confidential information is exposed, or a partner shares named client specifics without consent. The briefing is RESTRICTED, so credentials and unreleased work are the specific exposure to guard. Acknowledge, contain, and notify affected parties first, before any public comment. Reaffirm the co
Source capture ends here — full document held in the Maxxim engine
